summary: This advice column tackles several reader questions, with the main focus on a female math PhD student upset after being asked on a date publicly at an academic conference. Aunt Pythia counsels her to develop thicker skin, give the man benefit of the doubt, and not conflate awkwardness with injustice. Other questions address mathematical hagiographies (which she rails against as dead white male worship), the Ashley Madison hack (she's unconcerned about moral culpability), and a study showing women may be favored in tenure-track hiring (she accepts the data and urges calm analysis). Throughout, she advocates for pragmatism over idealism and refuses to treat minor slights as major offenses.
key_points: Being asked on a date awkwardly at a conference is uncomfortable but not an 'injustice' - women in math need thick skin for real battles ahead
Mathematical hero-worship and genius narratives (always about men) are harmful and off-putting to aspiring mathematicians who don't fit the mold
We cannot fully separate our mathematical selves from our personal/romantic selves - collaboration is inherently intimate
One study showing women favored at one hiring stage doesn't erase systemic sexism elsewhere in the pipeline
On Ashley Madison: marriages 'ruined' by the hack were probably already troubled - nobody is forced to ruin their marriage over data
money_quotes: The thing about men (and women) is that nobody knows what they're doing, but mostly they're not trying to be bad people.
Having a crush on you is his problem, not yours.
You're going to need a thicker skin to be a woman warrior in mathematics. Sad but true. Save the word 'injustice' for when it's really needed.
When is NPR going to realize that mathematicians are just people who like puzzles?

Maybe I became like this from all the fucking mathematical hagiographies of dead white men that were so unlike me that I simply turned it off inside me in order to be able to imagine myself as a successful mathematician.
Fuck that idolatry. I would never give my kids that crap to read.
I figure many of the marriages that are going to be 'ruined' because of Ashley Madison were kind of sucky anyway.
key_insight: Not every uncomfortable experience is an injustice, and conflating awkward social situations with systemic discrimination dilutes the fight against real barriers women face in mathematics.
